subject: Internal medicine
title: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
preambleMarkdown:
# Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is a clinical condition or syndrome in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: Imported Source Notes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
<!-- source: lib.md import start -->
Source: Core clinical source notes. Imported on 2026-07-14.

### Source Excerpt: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease

_Source lines: 5753-6342._

```text
Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
A. General Characteristics
1. There are two classic types of chronic obstructive pulmonary
disease (COPD): chronic bronchitis and emphysema (see Table
2-1 and Clinical Pearl 2-1)
a. Chronic bronchitis is a clinical diagnosis: chronic cough
productive of sputum for at least 3 months per year for at least
2 consecutive years, when other causes of chronic cough have
been excluded.

b. Emphysema is a pathologic diagnosis: permanent enlargement
of air spaces distal to terminal bronchioles due to destruction of
alveolar walls without fibrosis.

c. The two often coexist. Pure emphysema or pure chronic
bronchitis is rare.

d. There is also possible overlap between asthma and COPD,
although there is no consensus on the definition. Some may
have features of both diseases.

e. COPD is a leading cause of death in the United States.

Quick HIT
Pathology
Centrilobular emphysema:
- Most common type, seen in smokers (rarely in nonsmokers)
- Destruction limited to respiratory bronchioles (proximal acini)
with little change in distal acini

- Predilection for upper lung zones
Panlobular emphysema:
- Seen in patients with alpha 1-antitrypsin deficiency
- Destruction involves both proximal and distal acini
- Predilection for lung bases
1. Risk factors and causes
a. Tobacco smoke (indicated in almost 90% of COPD cases)
b. alpha 1-Antitrypsin deficiency-risk is even worse in combination
with smoking
c. Environmental factors (e.g., exposure to second-hand smoke,
fumes, or dust)
d. Chronic asthma-COPD overlap syndrome

## 2. Pathogenesis

a. Chronic bronchitis
- Excess mucus production narrows the airways; patients often
have a productive cough.

- Inflammation and scarring in airways, enlargement in
mucous glands, and smooth muscle hyperplasia lead to
obstruction.

d. Emphysema
- Destruction of alveolar walls is due to relative excess in
protease (elastase) activity, or relative deficiency of
antiprotease (alpha 1-antitrypsin) activity in the lung. Elastase is
released from polymorphonuclear neutrophils (PMNs) and
macrophages and digests human lung. This is inhibited by
alpha 1-antitrypsin.

- Tobacco smoke increases the number of activated PMNs and
macrophages, inhibits alpha 1-antitrypsin, and increases oxidative
stress on the lung by free-radical production.

Quick HIT
In COPD:
- The FEV1/FVC ratio is <0.70.

- FEV 1 is decreased.

- TLC is increased.

- Residual volume is increased.

B. Clinical Features

## 1. Symptoms

a. Any combination of cough, sputum production, and dyspnea (on

exertion or at rest, depending on severity) may be present.

b. Earliest symptom is exertional dyspnea, which may be dificult
to detect in patients with sedentary lifestyles who avoid
exertion.

2. Signs-the following may be present:
a. Normal physical examination in early disease
b. Prolonged expiratory time with pursed lip breathing
c. During auscultation, end-expiratory wheezes on forced
expiration, decreased breath sounds, crackles at the lung bases
d. Hyperresonance on percussion and distant heart sounds
e. Signs of cor pulmonale: hepatomegaly, distension of neck veins
with expiration
f. Signs of severe COPD:
- Tachypnea
- Tachycardia
- Cyanosis
- Use of accessory respiratory muscles
- Positions that relieve dyspnea: tripod position (leaning
forward with weight of arms on knees)
TABLE 2-1 COPD-Emphysema and Chronic Bronchitis
Predominant Emphysema ("Pink
Puffers")
Predominant Chronic Bronchitis ("Blue
Bloaters")
- Patients tend to be thin due to
increased energy expenditure
during breathing.

- When sitting, patients tend to
lean forward.

- Patients have a barrel chest
(increased AP diameter of
chest).

- Patients tend to be overweight and
cyanotic (secondary to chronic
hypercapnia and hypoxemia).

- Chronic cough and sputum production
are characteristic.

- Signs of cor pulmonale may be
present in severe or long-standing
disease.

Tachypnea with prolonged
expiration through pursed lips is
present.

Respiratory rate is normal or slightly
increased.

Patient is distressed and uses
accessory muscles (especially strap
muscles in neck).

Patient is in no apparent distress, and
there is no apparent use of accessory
muscles.

Quick HIT
FEV1 is the amount of air that can be forced out of the lungs in 1

second. The lower the FEV 1, the more dificulty one has breathing.

C. Diagnosis
1. PFT (spirometry)-see Table 2-2 and Figure 2-1
a. This is the definitive diagnostic test
b. Obstruction is evident based on the following:
- Decreased FEV 1 (the forced expiratory volume in the first
second of expiration)
AND
- FEV 1/FVC ratio <0.7 (FVC is the forced vital capacity, the
maximal amount of air that can be forcibly exhaled after
taking the deepest breath possible)
- GOLD staging is based on FEV 1. FEV 1 >=80% of predicted
value is mild disease, 50% to 80% is moderate disease, 30%
to 50% is severe disease, and <30% is very severe disease
- Disease severity is also clinically staged with GOLD "ABCD"
groups based on number of exacerbations requiring
hospitalization and degree of symptoms. This guides
treatment options
- Increased total lung capacity (TLC), residual volume, and
functional reserve capacity (FRC) (indicating air trapping)
(see Figure 2-2). Although COPD increases TLC, the excess
volume in the lung is not useful because it all becomes
residual volume and does not participate in gas exchange
- Decreased vital capacity
CLINICAL PEARL 2-1
Key Points in Taking History of COPD Patients
General
- History of cardiopulmonary diseases
- Smoking history (duration, intensity, current smoker)
- Family history-COPD, heart disease, asthma
- Occupation-industrial dusts, fumes
- Overall health
- History of respiratory infections-frequency, severity
- History of hospitalizations for COPD or exacerbations
- Pulmonary medications
Pulmonary Symptoms
- Dyspnea-quantify severity

- Cough
- Sputum production-quantity, quality, duration, hemoptysis
- Wheezing
Adapted with permission from Burton GG, Hodgkin JE, Ward JJ, eds. Respiratory Care
-A Guide to Clinical Practice. 4th ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 1997:1026. Table
28-3.

TABLE 2-2 Obstructive Versus Restrictive Lung Disease
Measurement 	Obstructive 	Restrictive
FEV1 	Low 	Normal or slightly low
FEV1 /FVC 	Low 	Normal or high
Peak expiratory flow rate 	Low 	Normal
Residual volume 	High 	Low, normal, or high
Total lung capacity 	High 	Low
Vital capacity 	Low 	Low
Quick HIT
To diagnose airway obstruction, one must have a normal or
increased TLC with a decreased FEV 1.

## 1. Chest radiograph (CXR)

a. Low sensitivity for diagnosing COPD; only severe, advanced
emphysema will show the typical changes, which include the
following:
- Hyperinflation, flattened diaphragm, enlarged retrosternal
space (see Figure 2-3)
- Diminished vascular markings
d. Useful in an acute exacerbation to rule out complications such
as pneumonia or pneumothorax
2. Measure alpha 1-antitrypsin levels in patients with a personal or family
history of premature emphysema (<=45 years old) or emphysema in
nonsmokers
3. Arterial blood gas (ABG) in those with SpO 2 <92% by pulse
oximetry, depressed level of consciousness, or acute exacerbation-
chronic PaCO 2 retention, decreased PaO 2, respiratory acidosis with
or without appropriate metabolic compensation

Quick HIT
COPD leads to chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic alkalosis
as compensation.

FIGURE
2-1 Lung volumes. IC, inspiratory capacity; ERV, expiratory reserve volume.

FIGURE
2-2 Flow volume loops. Examples of flow volume curves in a patient with
obstructive disease and a patient with restriction (diffuse interstitial
fibrosis), as compared with a healthy person. Volumes on the axis are
absolute volumes to better show the relation of flows (e.g., FEF 50% ) to
hyperinflation and restriction.

(Redrawn with permission from Humes HD, DuPont HL, Gardner LB, et al. Kelley's Textbook
of Internal Medicine. 4th ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2000:2580. Figure 387.2.)

A 42-year-old woman presents with progressive fatigue over the past few
weeks. She reports muscle fatigue, occasional double vision, and some
dificulty breathing. She has no relevant past medical history and takes
no medications. On examination, sustained upward gaze leads to muscle
fatigue and bilateral ptosis. Pulmonary function testing is performed and
is shown below.

FEV 1 	85% of predicted
Forced vital capacity (FVC) 	85% of predicted
FEV 1/FVC 	105% of
predicted
Total lung capacity (TLC) 	70% of predicted
Diffusion capacity of the lung for carbon
monoxide (DLCO)
100% of
predicted
Which of the following is responsible for this patient's pattern on
pulmonary function testing?

A. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
B. Neuromuscular disease
C. Pulmonary fibrosis
D. Pulmonary hemorrhage
- The answer is B: Neuromuscular disease. Myasthenia gravis is a
neuromuscular disease caused by autoantibodies directed against
postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors. Neuromuscular diseases can
show a restrictive pattern (decreased FEV 1 and FVC but normal/
increased FEV 1/FVC ratio) on pulmonary function testing that is
extrinsic to the lung itself and therefore will have a normal DLCO.

Examples include lower motor neuron disease (e.g., Guillain-Barre
syndrome), myasthenia gravis, Lambert-Eaton syndrome, muscular
dystrophies, chest wall deformities (e.g., scoliosis and pectus
carinatum), and obesity. (A) COPD will present with an obstructive
pattern on spirometry (decreased FEV 1/FVC ratio) with a decreased
DLCO (if there is a prominent component of emphysema). (C)
Pulmonary fibrosis is a type of interstitial lung disease, which will
show a restrictive pattern on spirometry as well as a decreased DLCO
from parenchymal destruction and scarring. (D) Pulmonary
hemorrhage would present with normal spirometry but an increased
DLCO, since the presence of red blood cells within the airways will
cause a rapid consumption of carbon monoxide during the test.

FIGURE
2-3 Chest radiographs showing a patient with COPD.

(A reprinted with permission from Daffner RH. Clinical Radiology: The Essentials. 3rd ed.:
Wolters Kluwer Health/Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2007:136. Figure 4.87A. B and C
reprinted with permission from Stern EJ, White CS. Chest Radiology Companion. Lippincott
Williams & Wilkins; 1999:189. Figure 13-1A,B.)

D. Treatment

## 1. Modalities

a. Smoking cessation-the most important intervention
- Disease progression is accelerated by continued smoking and
can be greatly slowed by its cessation.

- At around age 35, FEV 1 decreases approximately 25 to 30
mL/yr. In smokers, the rate of decline is faster (three- to
fourfold). If a smoker quits, the rate of decline of FEV 1 slows
to that of someone of the same age who has never smoked.

However,quitting does not result in complete reversal.

- Smoking cessation prolongs the survival rate but does
not reduce it to the level of someone who has never
smoked (see Figure 2-4).

- Respiratory symptoms improve within 1 year of quitting.

f. Inhaled antimuscarinic bronchodilators
- Slower onset of action than the beta-agonists, but last longer
- Short-acting muscarinic antagonists (SAMA): for example,
ipratropium
- Long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMA) for patients with
more severe symptoms: for example, tiotropium
j. Inhaled beta 2 -agonists (e.g., albuterol): bronchodilators
- Provide symptomatic relief
- Short-acting beta 2 -agonists (SABA, e.g., albuterol)
- Long-acting beta-agonists (LABA) for patients requiring
frequent use (e.g., salmeterol)
n. Combination of SABA with SAMA (SABA + SAMA, e.g.,

albuterol + ipratropium)
- More eficacious than either agent alone in bronchodilation
- Also helps with adherence to therapy (both medications in
one inhaler)
- Other combinations exist (e.g., LAMA + LABA such as
tiotropium-olodaterol) and may improve adherence
r. Inhaled corticosteroids (e.g., budesonide, fluticasone,
mometasone): anti-inflammatory
- May minimally slow down the decrease in FEV 1 over time;
however, many studies have failed to show any benefit in
pulmonary function. May also increase rates of pulmonary
infection
- Typically used in combination with a long-acting
bronchodilator for patients with significant symptoms or
repeated exacerbations
- Most useful in patients with serum eosinophils >300 cells/
L
v. Theophylline (oral)-role is controversial
- May improve mucociliary clearance and central respiratory
drive.

- Narrow therapeutic index, so serum levels must be
monitored.

- More side effects than other bronchodilators. Occasionally
used for patients with refractory COPD.

z. Phosphodiesterase-4 inhibitors (e.g., roflumilast)
- Promote smooth muscle relaxation and decrease
inflammation.

- May reduce risk of frequent exacerbations.

ac. Oxygen therapy
- Shown to improve survival and quality of life in patients
with COPD and chronic hypoxemia.

- Some patients need continuous oxygen, whereas others only
require it during exertion or sleep. Get an ABG to determine
need for oxygen (see Quick Hit).

- Long-standing hypoxemia may lead to pulmonary
hypertension (HTN) and ultimately cor pulmonale.

Continuous oxygen therapy for >=18 hrs/day has been
shown to reduce mortality in patients with these
complications by controlling pulmonary HTN.

ag. Pulmonary rehabilitation-education, exercise, physiotherapy:
A major goal is to improve exercise tolerance. Pulmonary
rehabilitation improves functional status and quality of life, and
may reduce mortality after hospital discharge

ah. Vaccination
- Influenza vaccination annually for all patients.

- Vaccination against Streptococcus pneumoniae-should be
offered to all adult patients with COPD, regardless of age.

Offer once before age 65, then once over age 65.

ak. Antibiotics are given for acute exacerbations (see below)-
increased sputum production in volume or change in character
or worsening shortness of breath (SOB)
al. Surgery-may be beneficial in selected patients; carefully weigh
potential benefits with risks. Options include the following:
- Lung volume resection surgery
- Lung transplantation
Quick HIT
Clinical monitoring of COPD patients entails the following:
- Serial FEV1 measurements-this has the highest predictive
value
- Pulse oximetry
- Severity of symptoms: exercise tolerance, cough, sputum,
breathlessness
FIGURE
2-4 Smoking and COPD. Age-related rate of decline in lung function (FEV1.0 )
in a nonsmoker (top) and susceptible smoker (bottom). The dashed lines
indicate the beneficial effects of smoking cessation with moderate and
severe disease. The accelerated decline in lung function approaches the

normal rate, significantly delaying the onset of disability and death.

(Redrawn from Fletcher C, Peto R. The natural history of chronic airflow obstruction. Br Med
J. 1977;1(6077):1645-1648 with permission from BMJ Publishing Group Ltd.)

Quick HIT
Smoking cessation and home oxygen therapy are the only
interventions shown to consistently lower mortality.

Quick HIT
- Treat COPD with bronchodilators (antimuscarinics, beta 2-agonists,
or both).

- Give steroids and antibiotics for acute exacerbations.

Quick HIT
If persistent COPD symptoms, review medication adherence and
inhaler technique.

Quick HIT
Systemic glucocorticoids are only used for acute exacerbations and
should not be used for long-term treatment, even for patients with
severe COPD.

Quick HIT
Criteria for continuous or intermittent long-term oxygen therapy in
COPD:
- PaO2 55 mm Hg OR
- O2 saturation <=88% (pulse oximetry), either at rest or during
exercise OR
- PaO2 55 to 59 mm Hg plus polycythemia or evidence of cor
pulmonale
- Note that the above must be consistent findings despite optimal
medical therapy
1. Treatment guidelines-stepwise therapy based on severity of
symptoms and prior number of exacerbations or COPD-related
hospitalizations (see Figure 2-5)

a. Low risk of exacerbation (0 to 1 exacerbations in past year)
- Begin with a short-acting bronchodilator (antimuscarinic
and/or beta-agonist) as needed in a metered-dose inhaler (MDI)
formulation (with spacer to improve delivery).

- Add daily long-acting bronchodilator, if more symptomatic.

d. High risk of exacerbation (2 or more exacerbations per year)
- Regular use of long-acting bronchodilator (antimuscarinic
and/or beta-agonist)
- Add inhaled corticosteroid, if more symptomatic
- Consider additional agents (roflumilast or theophylline)
- Continuous oxygen therapy (if patient is hypoxemic)
- Pulmonary rehabilitation

FIGURE
2-5 Treatment guidelines for chronic obstructive lung disease, based on the
revised 2017 Global Initiative for Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD)
guidelines.

(Reprinted with permission from Thompson WR. ACSM's Clinical Exercise Physiology.

Wolters Kluwer; 2019. Figure 9.9.)

1. Acute COPD exacerbation. Definition: Increased dyspnea, sputum
production, and/or cough. Acute COPD exacerbation can lead to
acute respiratory failure requiring hospitalization, and possibly
mechanical ventilation; potentially fatal

a. Bronchodilators (beta 2-agonist) alone or in combination with
antimuscarinics are first-line therapy.

b. Systemic corticosteroids are used for patients requiring
hospitalization. A short course of oral prednisone is common
practice.

c. Antibiotics (azithromycin, doxycycline, or fluoroquinolones):
Reserved for those with moderate or severe exacerbations
requiring hospitalization. Beware of common adverse effects
(e.g., QT prolongation with azithromycin, fluoroquinolones).

d. Supplemental oxygen is used to keep O 2 saturation 88% to 92%.

Start with a nasal cannula; a face mask may need to be used.

- If SaO 2 is >92%, the patient is at risk of CO 2 retention from
worsening ventilation/perfusion (V/Q) mismatch and the
Haldane effect (not from loss of hypoxic respiratory drive).

f. Noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NPPV) (bilevel
positive airway pressure [BiPAP] or continuous positive airway
pressure [CPAP]): Studies have shown a benefit in acute
exacerbations. It may decrease the likelihood of respiratory
failure requiring invasive mechanical ventilation.

g. Intubation and mechanical ventilation may be required if the
above do not stabilize the patient. Intubate, if increasing
respiratory rate (RR), increasing PaCO 2, and worsening acidosis.

Quick HIT
- One of the main treatment goals for COPD patients is to reduce
the number and severity of exacerbations they experience each
year.

- Pulmonary infection (most commonly viruses, followed by
bacteria such as S. pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae,
Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Moraxella catarrhalis) is one of the
main precipitants of a COPD exacerbation.

Quick HIT
If a patient presents with COPD exacerbation, the following steps
are appropriate:
- CXR
- beta 2 -Agonist and antimuscarinic inhalers
- Systemic corticosteroids
- Antibiotics
- Supplemental oxygen
- NPPV, if needed (see Clinical Pearl 2-9)

E. Complications
1. Acute exacerbations-most common causes are infection,
nonadherence with therapy, and cardiac disease
2. Secondary polycythemia (Hct >55% in men or >47% in women)
-compensatory response to chronic hypoxemia
3. Pulmonary HTN and cor pulmonale-may occur in patients with
severe, long-standing COPD who have chronic hypoxemia
A 59-year-old woman with a history of hypertension and COPD presents
to the hospital with shortness of breath with an increased cough and
sputum production. She has a temperature of 37.6 C, blood pressure of
138/88 mm Hg, heart rate of 96 beats/min, respiratory rate of 28
breaths/min, and oxygen saturation of 84% on room air. She is anxious
and is using respiratory accessory muscles to breathe. There is
hyperresonance to percussion along both lung fields with wheezes and
rhonchi heard bilaterally. The CXR does not show any infiltrates.

Which of the following is NOT an appropriate first-line treatment for the
patient at this time?

A. Oxygenation
B. Methylprednisolone
C. Azithromycin
D. Inhaled corticosteroids
- The answer is D: Inhaled corticosteroids. This patient is
presenting with a history of COPD with an acute exacerbation, which
is defined as an increase in any of the chronic symptoms of COPD
(increased frequency/severity of cough, increased dyspnea, or
increased sputum amount or change in color). The majority of acute
exacerbations are caused by respiratory infections. (A, B, C)
Management of acute COPD exacerbations should be differentiated
from the management of chronic disease. Important therapies during
an exacerbation include oxygen administration targeting an SaO 2 of
88% to 92%, inhaled bronchodilators (anticholinergics and beta 2 -
agonists), systemic corticosteroids, and antibiotics. Antibiotics show
some benefit in COPD exacerbations. (D) Although inhaled
corticosteroids are useful in the chronic treatment of COPD, there is
no evidence for their use in acute exacerbations and should not be
used instead of systemic corticosteroids.
```

<!-- source: lib.md import end -->

<!-- source: step-up-emergency-medicine import start -->
Source: Step-Up to Emergency Medicine. Imported on 2026-07-14.

### Source Excerpt: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease

_Source lines: 7178-7344._

```text
III. CHRONIC OBSTRUCTIVE PULMONARY DISEASE
A. Discussion
1. Definition. COPD is a syndrome characterized by chronic dyspnea and expira-
tory airflow obstruction as a result of increased resistance or decreased airway
caliber throughout the small bronchi and bronchioles. There are two types of
COPD.

a. Emphysema is permanent, abnormal enlargement of the terminal bronchi-
oles accompanied by destruction of the bronchoalveolar lining.

b. Chronic bronchitis is defined by a productive cough on most days for at
least 3 months per year for at least 2 consecutive years.

2. Incidence. COPD is the fifth leading cause of death in the United States.

3. Causes. Smoking is the leading cause of COPD. Cystic fibrosis, $1 -antitrypsin
deficiency, and some occupational exposures predispose to the development of
COPD.

Quick 	HIT
Mechanical ventilation does
not relieve the airflow ob-
struction, and high peak air-
way pressures are common.

88 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Pulmonary Emergencies
B. Clinical features. Exacerbations are what bring most patients with COPD to the ED.

## 1. Symptoms

a. Wheezing , dyspnea on exertion, and cough are the most common
symptoms. Wheezing may not be audible if the patient's airflow is very
poor.

b. Accessory muscle use may be seen in patients with moderate to severe
COPD exacerbations.

c. Pursed-lip breathing may be present.

d. Altered mental status, severely labored breathing, and extreme fatigue
signal impending respiratory arrest.

2. Physical examination findings. Signs of right-sided CHF (cor pulmonale)
may be present and include jugular venous distention, peripheral edema, and
hepatomegaly.

C. Differential diagnoses include CHF, upper airway obstruction, foreign body aspi-
ration, pulmonary embolism, an anaphylactic reaction, pneumonia, asthma, toxic
inhalation, and pneumothorax.

D. Evaluation. The evaluation of a patient with COPD exacerbation is similar to that
for patients with asthma.

1. Pulmonary function testing. The PEFR can assist in monitoring response to
treatment; however, it is not as useful as an evaluation tool for patients with
COPD (as compared with those with asthma) because acute bronchospasm
contributes less to dysfunction in patients with COPD. Instead, forced expira-
tory volume in 1 second as a percentage of predicted is used (Table 3-2).

2. Pulse oximetry can be used to evaluate the degree of hypoxia.

3. Chest radiographs should be considered for patients with tachycardia, tachy-
pnea, hypoxia, fever, a focal lung examination, age " 65 years old, or symp-
toms not responsive to bronchodilator therapy.

4. ABG. An ABG should be obtained on patients who do not respond to bron-
chodilator therapy or in whom acute or serious respiratory failure is suspected.

When interpreting the ABG, it is important to remember that patients with
COPD may retain carbon dioxide on a chronic basis. If a patient has an el-
evated Pco2 in the face of a normal blood pH, then this usually represents
chronic carbon dioxide retention (with metabolic compensation) rather than
acute respiratory failure. However, if the Pco 2 is elevated and the blood pH is
low, the patient is in respiratory failure.

5. Laboratory studies
a. The hematocrit may be elevated secondary to increased erythrocytosis.

b. The serum bicarbonate level may be elevated, suggesting that a secondary
metabolic alkalosis is compensating for the chronic respiratory acidosis.

E. Therapy
1. Oxygen should be administered to all patients with COPD. Some patients with
COPD with a chronically elevated Pco 2 have lost their sensitivity to hyper-
carbia and thus depend on the hypoxic drive to stimulate their respirations.

Therefore, the minimum amount of oxygen that is needed to maintain the
oxygen saturation at 90% should be administered.

Quick 	HIT
Only 15% of smokers will
develop COPD.

Quick 	HIT
If an excessive concentra-
tion of oxygen is adminis-
tered to these patients in an
attempt to normalize their
oxygen saturation, it may de-
press their respiratory drive
and lead to hypoventilation
and respiratory failure.

Stage 	Signs and Symptoms 	Initial FEV 1 as % of Predicted
I. Mild 	Dyspnea with activity 	FEV1 " 80% predicted
II. Moderate 	Dyspnea interferes with activity 	FEV1 50%-79% predicted
III. Severe 	Dyspnea at rest 	FEV1 30%-49% predicted
IV. Life-threatening 	Too dyspneic to speak 	FEV1 # 30% predicted
FEV1, forced expiratory volume in 1 second.

COPD Severity	TABLE 3-2
P U L M O N A R Y E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	89

Pulmonary Emergencies
2. Pharmacologic therapy
a. Inhaled !2 agonists are first-line agents for the treatment of acute exacerba-
tions of COPD. Dosage and administration is the same as that for patients
with asthma (see II.E.2.a.i).

b. Inhaled anticholinergic agents are the second-line agents for the treatment
of acute COPD exacerbations. Dosage and administration is the same as
that for patients with asthma (see II.E.2.a.iii).

c. Corticosteroids should be considered for patients with COPD who do
not respond rapidly to inhaled !2 agonists or anticholinergics. The dos-
ing and administration is the same as that for patients with asthma
(see II.E.2.a.ii).

d. Antibiotics should be administered if there is evidence of infection, such
as increased sputum production or change in sputum character. Choices
should be directed at the common pathogens, namely, Streptococcus pneu-
moniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Moraxella catarrhalis.

3. Intubation and mechanical ventilation are generally indicated for patients
with impending respiratory failure. Unlike asthmatic patients, patients with
COPD with mild acute elevations in Pco 2 and a decreased blood pH can some-
times be managed without intubation, as long as they do not demonstrate
central nervous system or cardiovascular dysfunction and are not severely
fatigued.

a. Noninvasive positive-pressure ventilatory support can often assist ventila-
tion and improve a patient's clinical condition enough to obviate intubation.

Positive pressure is delivered through either a nasal or face mask using a
ventilator or a bilevel positive airway pressure system.

b. Ketamine is not a preferred induction agent in these patients because
many of them have concomitant ischemic heart disease.

F. 	Disposition. Patients with COPD exacerbations are more likely to be admitted
than patients with an asthma exacerbation because reversible disease accounts for
a smaller part of their clinical presentation.

1. Admission. Pneumonia, CHF, or other existing comorbid conditions are
indications for hospital admission. Persistent hypoxemia or worsening
hypercarbia with concomitant respiratory acidosis (pH #7.30) require ICU
admission.

2. Discharge. Patients may be discharged to home if they have made a significant
clinical improvement and are near their baseline respiratory status.
```
